slides.headline: The Conditional Activation Model
slides.subhead: Uncovering Policy Demand Responses to AI-Driven Automation
slides.notes: Open with the aggregate null puzzle: why do information experiments on automation risk so often produce null or conflicting effects? The CAM framework resolves this as a composition effect.

blocks.label: Author
blocks.text: Ignacio Urbina · Stony Brook University · April 2026

slides.headline: Automation exposure is widespread, yet experimental evidence on policy demand remains inconclusive.
slides.subhead: Frey & Osborne (2017) estimate 47% of U.S. occupations face high automation risk. Public awareness is high (Pew 2023), but experiments linking risk information to policy preferences yield mixed results.
slides.notes: Frame as a scientific puzzle. The disconnect between public awareness and experimental null effects is what motivates the paper.

items.title: Structural labor market shift
items.detail: Routine cognitive and manual tasks face increasing automation pressure

items.title: Policy demand channel
items.detail: Standard models predict risk exposure → demand for retraining, regulation, redistribution

items.title: Empirical gap
items.detail: Experimental tests of this channel produce inconsistent or null aggregate effects

items.label: High-risk occupations
items.detail: of U.S. employment (Frey & Osborne 2017)

items.label: Public concern
items.detail: major concern about AI workforce impact (Pew 2023)

slides.headline: Three traditions converge: personal risk information should increase policy demand.
slides.subhead: Self-interest theory, threat-motivated cognition, and egotropic economic voting each predict that learning one's occupation faces automation risk should increase support for protective policies.
slides.notes: Self-interest: Meltzer & Richard (1981). Threat motivation: Albertson & Gadarian (2015). Egotropic: Kinder & Kiewiet (1981).

items.label: Self-interest
items.detail: Workers facing losses seek compensatory policy (Meltzer & Richard 1981)

items.label: Threat-motivated cognition
items.detail: Threat perception focuses attention on protection (Albertson & Gadarian 2015)

items.label: Egotropic voting
items.detail: Individual economic exposure drives policy demand (Kinder & Kiewiet 1981)

slides.headline: Experimental results are inconsistent: null, positive, and opposing effects coexist.
slides.subhead: Studies examining conditional effects find heterogeneity that washes out in the aggregate.
slides.notes: The pivot comes from Arntz et al. (2024), who find opposing subgroup effects that cancel at the aggregate level. Wu (2022) and Im (2023) provide further evidence for conditional moderation by ideology, education, and prior beliefs.

items.title: Null aggregate effects are common
items.detail: Zhang & Hou (2022), Magistro et al. (2024): personalized risk information → null ATE on policy preferences

items.title: Positive findings exist but with different designs
items.detail: Gallego et al. (2022): distinct treatment and sample yield positive effects

items.title: Subgroup heterogeneity drives cancellation
items.detail: Arntz et al. (2024): opposing subgroup effects cancel in the aggregate. Wu (2022), Im (2023): effects moderated by ideology, education, prior beliefs

slides.headline: The unconditional ATE averages over heterogeneous subgroups — the estimand is misspecified.
slides.subhead: Null aggregate effects may reflect composition rather than preference stability. The relevant estimand conditions on trust type and activation state.
slides.notes: Pivotal slide. Reframe from ATE to CATE.

left.label: Standard estimand
left.text: E[Y(1) − Y(0)]: unconditional ATE across full sample

right.label: Proposed estimand
right.text: E[Y(1) − Y(0) | S, Gap, Anxiety]: CATE stratified by trust type and activation state

slides.headline: Two sequential gates: source trust (discrete) and joint activation (Gap × Anxiety).
slides.subhead: Source trust operates as a binary filter, not a continuous moderator. Activation requires the joint presence of informational shock and dispositional vigilance.
slides.notes: Source trust: discrete accept/reject. Activation: neither gap alone nor anxiety alone sufficient. Below threshold: cool cognition.

items.title: Source trust: discrete classification
items.detail: Latent mixture separates Trusters from Skeptics — not a continuous moderator

items.title: Activation: joint requirement
items.detail: Neither belief gap nor trait anxiety alone is sufficient to trigger policy search

items.title: Below-threshold pathway
items.detail: Beliefs update without policy preference change (cool cognition)

slides.headline: The two-gate structure predicts three qualitatively distinct cognitive pathways.
slides.subhead: Each pathway corresponds to a different exit point in the information-processing sequence. A population-level null ATE is the expected signature when the three pathways are mixed.
slides.notes: This is a theoretical slide — no empirical numbers yet. The three pathways are derived from the gate structure, not from data. The key theoretical claim is that aggregate nulls are predicted by the model under heterogeneous mixing, not anomalous.

items.label: 1. Source rejection
items.detail: Information fails the trust gate; no downstream processing regardless of gap magnitude or anxiety level

items.label: 2. Cool cognition
items.detail: Information is accepted and beliefs update, but insufficient vigilance to initiate policy search

items.label: 3. Activated persuasion
items.detail: Both gates cleared; sustained elaboration of threat translates into demand for protective policy

blocks.label: Prediction
blocks.text: If the three pathways are mixed in the population, the unconditional ATE will understate the responsive subgroup — producing the null or weak aggregate effects observed in prior work.

slides.headline: Pre-registered between-subjects experiment, N = 901 U.S. workers.
slides.subhead: Three-step belief correction protocol with individualized treatment intensity. 475 treatment, 426 control; block randomized by occupation.
slides.notes: Workers compress beliefs toward 30-40 regardless of actual risk. Metacognitive failure: r = -0.004 between certainty and accuracy.

items: Reveal expert estimate; display belief gap

left.label: Design property
left.text: Treatment intensity varies naturally via belief gap, creating continuous within-treatment variation.

items.title: Outcome
items.detail: Policy demand index: 5 items, 7-pt scale (ω = 0.85)

items.title: Source trust
items.detail: 5-point item, post-treatment only

items.title: Belief gap
items.detail: Expert percentile − subjective prior

items.title: Trait anxiety
items.detail: 4-item scale, pre-treatment (ω = 0.90)

slides.headline: Five-item policy demand index and three moderators operationalize the two-gate structure.
slides.subhead: Policy demand: 5 items, 7-pt scale (ω = 0.85). Trust and gap are post-treatment; anxiety is pre-treatment.
slides.notes: The battery deliberately spans redistribution, regulation, retraining, labor protections, and employment limits to avoid anchoring to a single ideological frame. Trust is a single post-treatment item — a limitation, but necessary to avoid contaminating control. Gap is computed (expert minus subjective), not self-reported. Anxiety uses a validated Big Five facet scale.

cells: 5 items, 7-pt scale (ω = 0.85): redistribution, regulation, retraining, labor protections, employment limits

blocks.label: Asymmetry
blocks.text: Trust and gap are post-treatment (treated only); anxiety is pre-treatment (all respondents). The BFMM handles the resulting collider structure.

slides.headline: The treatment successfully shifted beliefs; belief gap provides continuous within-treatment variation.
slides.subhead: 93.4% comprehension pass rate. Mean gap = 2.44 (SD = 36), range [−89, +85].
slides.notes: Workers compress beliefs toward 30–40 regardless of actual risk. The metacognitive failure (r = −0.004 between certainty and accuracy) means subjective confidence does not track objective accuracy. This is what makes the gap a genuine informational shock rather than a confirmation exercise.

items.label: Comprehension
items.detail: passed methodology check

items.label: Certainty × accuracy
items.detail: Metacognitive failure: confidence does not track accuracy

slides.headline: Observed trust is a post-treatment collider; naive conditioning introduces bias.
slides.subhead: The BFMM uses principal stratification: Gap and Gap² block the collider path and isolate dispositional trust.
slides.notes: Trust measured only for treated AND caused by both disposition and treatment. The CATE function is type-specific: Skeptics are constrained to have coefficients no larger than Trusters (non-negativity).

steps.title: Problem
steps.detail: Gap → Q_obs ← S creates collider bias

steps.title: Block
steps.detail: Gap, Gap² block the collider path

slides.headline: Unconditional ATE is positive and robust across estimators (d ≈ 0.24–0.27).
slides.subhead: All specifications converge. The BFMM reproduces the ATE while decomposing it into type-specific components.
slides.notes: Starting point — aggregate effect exists but understates responsive subgroup by factor 2.5.

slides.headline: Source trust operates as a discrete gate: Skeptic effects are null across all moderator values.
slides.notes: Even Skeptics with large gaps AND high anxiety show null effects (all P(β > 0) < 0.30). Trusters show positive effects across all moderation coefficients (all P(β > 0) > 0.92). Inconsistent with continuous moderator models. Estimates from 24,000 posterior draws (6 chains × 4,000 post-warmup). All R-hat < 1.001.

blocks.label: Finding
blocks.text: Source rejection functions as a strict binary filter: all Skeptic moderation coefficients have P(β > 0) < 0.30; all Truster coefficients have P(β > 0) > 0.92.

slides.headline: Among Trusters, effects are jointly activated by belief gap and trait anxiety (β₃ = 0.13, P > 0: 0.92).
slides.subhead: Activation threshold at ≈ 20th percentile of trait anxiety. Below threshold, large gaps produce null CATEs.
slides.notes: Neither gap nor anxiety alone sufficient. All pairwise contrasts P(Δ > 0) > 0.97.

items.title: Activation threshold
items.detail: ≈ 20th percentile of trait anxiety (z ≈ −0.84)

items.title: Below threshold
items.detail: Large gaps → null CATEs (cool cognition)

items.title: Above threshold
items.detail: CATEs scale linearly with Gap × Anxiety

items.title: Maximum CATE
items.detail: 2.5× the unconditional ATE

slides.headline: Null aggregate effects reflect composition across heterogeneous pathways, not preference stability.
slides.subhead: The two-gate architecture generalizes to any persuasion domain with expert sources and dispositional moderators.
slides.notes: Scope: U.S. workers, liberal skew, single trust item, immediate measurement, F&O 2017 estimates. Generalization domains: climate, vaccines, trade.

items: Theoretical: two-gate model resolves conflicting results as composition effects across three pathways.
Methodological: BFMM addresses latent classification with collider-biased post-treatment moderators.
Empirical: maximum CATE (d = 0.61) exceeds ATE by factor 2.5; robust across specifications.

right.label: Scope
right.text: N = 901 U.S. workers · 62% Democrat · immediate measurement · F&O 2017 estimates. Generalizes to climate, vaccines, trade.
